architecture.summary: Coconut (Chain of Continuous Thought) modifies standard LLM decoding to reason in latent space rather than language space. During reasoning, the model switches between 'language mode' (standard autoregressive token generation) and 'latent mode' (feeding the last hidden state directly as the next input embedding, bypassing the language model head and embedding layer). Special tokens <bot> and <eot> mark latent reasoning boundaries. A multi-stage training curriculum gradually replaces language reasoning chain tokens with continuous thought vectors, supervising latent reasoning with language CoT data. The continuous thoughts remain fully differentiable and support backpropagation. At inference, the model may output final answers in language or maintain continuous latent reasoning steps for planning.
trained_components: continuous thought representations (last hidden states fed back as embeddings)
model weights updated via gradient descent on remaining language tokens

biblio.title: Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space

claims.statement: Continuous thoughts can encode multiple alternative next reasoning steps, allowing the model to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) to solve the problem, rather than prematurely committing to a single deterministic path like CoT
Coconut outperforms CoT on ProsQA logical reasoning task requiring substantial planning ability
Coconut achieves comparable accuracy to CoT on ProntoQA logical reasoning while generating significantly fewer tokens
Coconut generates 9.0 tokens on ProntoQA versus 92.5 tokens for CoT while maintaining comparable accuracy
Coconut generates 14.2 tokens on ProsQA versus 49.4 tokens for CoT while achieving higher accuracy (97.0% vs 77.5%)
On GSM8k math reasoning, Coconut achieves 34.1% accuracy with 8.2 tokens, outperforming No-CoT (16.5% accuracy) while generating fewer tokens than CoT (42.9% accuracy with 25.0 tokens)
Coconut trained without multi-stage curriculum (w/o curriculum) performs no better than No-CoT baseline, achieving only 14.4% accuracy on GSM8k versus 34.1% with curriculum

The model's estimated value function shows nodes with lower heights (closer to leaf nodes) receive more accurate and definitive probability evaluations, whereas nodes with greater heights exhibit more ambiguous evaluations, reflecting increased uncertainty
